skill.name: azure-mgmt-arizeaiobservabilityeval-dotnet
skill.description: Guides .NET developers through Azure Resource Manager operations for Arize AI Observability and Evaluation resources.
skill.summary: Manage Arize AI Observability and Evaluation organizations on Azure with the .NET ARM SDK.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution hits, but review found Markdown code fences, inline package names, and C# SDK examples rather than executable skill logic. The hardcoded URLs are documentation links, and the critical combination heuristic is not supported by evidence. The only residual concern is guidance that references Azure client secret environment variables.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences, inline type names, package names, or C# SDK examples. No evidence found of skill-side dynamic command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is visibly Markdown or C# sample code, not executable Ruby backtick syntax. The file contains no helper script or runtime wrapper that would execute these examples.

low_findings.title: Azure Client Secret Environment Variable Is Documented
low_findings.description: The skill documents AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_CLIENT_ID, and AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET. This is legitimate Azure SDK setup guidance, but secrets in environment variables can be exposed through shell history, process dumps, or logs if handled carelessly.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential variable names are explicitly present, including a client secret placeholder. The risk is limited because the skill does not read, transmit, or store those values.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Reference Links
low_findings.description: The reported network indicators are documentation links to NuGet, the Azure SDK repository, and Arize AI. No evidence found of network calls, telemetry, download execution, or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in a Links section and are not used by code. They point to expected vendor or package documentation resources.

low_findings.title: Blocker Heuristics Are Not Supported by File Context
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and system reconnaissance findings do not match the reviewed content. Line 3 begins front matter text, and line 191 recommends GetIfExistsAsync for conditional resource lookup.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain cryptographic algorithms or host reconnaissance commands. They are descriptive front matter and Azure SDK best practice text.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed
low_findings.description: The scanner combined code execution, network, and credential indicators into a critical heuristic. Manual review found documentation snippets and links only, with no malicious workflow or prompt injection attempt.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All components of the heuristic are explained by benign SDK documentation. No evidence found of code that combines credentials with external network transfer.

content.user_title: Manage Arize AI Resources on Azure
content.value_statement: Developers need reliable examples for managing Arize AI organizations through Azure Resource Manager. This skill provides focused .NET SDK guidance for create, read, update, delete, and error handling workflows.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install the Azure.ResourceManager.ArizeAIObservabilityEval package.
Explains DefaultAzureCredential setup for Azure Resource Manager clients.
Provides examples for creating Arize AI organization resources.
Covers get, list, update, delete, and direct resource access workflows.
Lists key SDK resource, collection, data, patch, marketplace, and SSO types.
Includes basic Azure RequestFailedException handling patterns.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not run Azure operations by itself.
It assumes the Azure SDK package version and API version shown in the skill.
It does not cover every Azure tenant, billing, or Marketplace approval scenario.
It uses placeholder user and subscription values that must be replaced.

use_cases.title: Create Azure Arize Organizations
use_cases.description: Build a .NET workflow that provisions an Arize AI Observability and Evaluation organization through Azure Resource Manager.

use_cases.title: Operate Existing Resources
use_cases.description: List, inspect, tag, and delete Arize AI organization resources in a resource group or subscription.

use_cases.title: Integrate ML Observability
use_cases.description: Connect Azure Marketplace Arize AI resources with a .NET service that manages observability infrastructure.

prompt_templates.title: Install the SDK
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to install the .NET Azure Resource Manager package for Arize AI Observability and Evaluation.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Organization
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create an Arize AI Observability and Evaluation organization in Azure with the .NET ARM SDK.

prompt_templates.title: Manage Resource Lifecycle
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a .NET workflow to get, list, update tags, and delete Arize AI organization resources in Azure.

prompt_templates.title: Harden Error Handling
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Azure Arize AI management flow and improve retry, not-found handling, and long-running operation handling.

output_examples.input: I need to create an Arize AI organization from a .NET service.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan covering package installation, Azure authentication, resource group lookup, organization data setup, and long-running create operation handling.

output_examples.input: How do I check whether an Arize organization exists before reading it?
output_examples.output: Guidance that recommends existence checks or nullable lookup methods before retrieving the organization, with a short explanation of when each approach fits.

output_examples.input: What types should I know for updates and Marketplace configuration?
output_examples.output: A concise mapping of resource, collection, data, patch, marketplace, offer, user, and SSO types to their expected use.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential and prefer managed identity when available.
Treat Marketplace subscription and user contact values as environment-specific configuration.
Handle long-running operations, retries, and not-found responses explicitly.
anti_patterns: Do not commit real Azure client secrets, subscription identifiers, or user contact data.
Do not hardcode production resource group names in reusable libraries.
Do not assume delete and create operations finish immediately.

faq.question: What does this skill help me build?
faq.answer: It helps you use the .NET Azure Resource Manager SDK to manage Arize AI Observability and Evaluation organization resources.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Azure commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation and examples that a developer can adapt in a .NET application.

faq.question: Which authentication approach does it show?
faq.answer: It shows DefaultAzureCredential, which can work with managed identity, developer login, or service principal configuration.

faq.question: Can I use it for production code?
faq.answer: Yes, but you should add your own configuration, validation, logging, retry policy, and secret management controls.

faq.question: Does it cover all Arize AI features?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on Azure Resource Manager organization management, not the full Arize AI product API.

faq.question: What security care is needed?
faq.answer: Keep Azure secrets out of source control, prefer managed identity, and avoid logging credentials or personal contact data.
